title: Turning a hot compost heap
subtitle: What turning does, when to do it, and how to check the heap is actually hot
excerpt: A hot compost heap reaches 55-70°C in the first week if it is correctly constructed. Turning moves the cool outer material into the hot centre, aerates the heap to restore oxygen for microbial activity, and extends the hot phase. A compost thermometer tells you when to turn and when the heap has finished its active phase.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on hot composting and turning frequency. Charles Dowding no-dig composting notes. WRAP household composting guide on thermophilic composting temperatures.

glossaryTerms.definition: Active composting where the heap reaches 55-70°C due to microbial activity. Kills weed seeds and pathogens. Requires a minimum heap volume of approximately 1 cubic metre, correct moisture, and a balanced carbon-to-nitrogen ratio. Produces finished compost in 8-16 weeks.

Heat-loving bacteria that operate at 45-70°C and drive the hot phase of composting. They consume nitrogen-rich material rapidly and produce heat as a byproduct. When oxygen is depleted or the nitrogen is exhausted, the heap cools and mesophilic (lower-temperature) organisms take over to finish the process.

content.text: A correctly built heap heats up within 2-4 days of construction as

at 55°C for three days kills most weed seeds and plant pathogens. A compost thermometer (a long-probe bimetallic thermometer, approximately 400-600 mm probe length) pushed into the centre of the heap gives an accurate reading. The outer 150 mm of the heap stays at ambient temperature regardless of the core temperature.

Check the heap temperature every 2-3 days during the active phase. Turn when the core temperature drops below 45°C, which indicates that oxygen has been consumed or the active microbial zone has shrunk. In practice, a fresh heap needs turning every 5-10 days for the first month. After turning, temperature typically rises back to 55-65°C within 24-48 hours as oxygen is restored. If temperature does not recover after turning, the heap is either too dry (add water) or too low in nitrogen (add fresh grass clippings or green material).

Fork the outer layers off first, piling them to one side. Then fork the hot core material into the empty bay or new pile, building a new pile and placing the outer layers back in the centre. The goal is to invert the heap: material that was on the outside ends up in the middle, where it will be exposed to the highest temperatures. Add water during turning if the material feels dry: it should feel like a wrung-out sponge throughout.
When the active phase is over

After 3-5 turns over 6-8 weeks, the heap will stop reheating after turning: the easily available nitrogen has been consumed. At this point, the pile moves into the maturation phase, where worms, fungi, and mesophilic organisms continue breaking down the remaining material at ambient temperature. Leave to mature for 4-8 weeks. Finished compost is dark brown to black, crumbly, smells of earth, and shows no recognisable inputs except possibly the toughest woody stems.
